introSection: The Beast is a wooden terrain roller coaster located at Kings Island in Mason, Ohio, USA. It is currently the longest operating roller coaster in the United States, as well as the longest wooden roller coaster in the world. The ride takes up 35 acres ( km/h) of land. The trains were built by Philadelphia Toboggan Coasters.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
Kings Island unveiled the roller coaster, at the time unnamed, on July 10, 1978. According to the park, the project had been in planning for three years.

Lead engineers Al Collins and Jeff Gramke, with some consulting assistance from legendary designer John C. Allen, headed the project. Charles Dinn, director of maintenance and construction, led construction efforts and enlisted help from Curtis D. Summers for the footings and helix finale. Late design modifications were made to resolve issues discovered during testing, delaying plans to build an enclosure over the helix until the following off-season.

A ride car was displayed at the 1978 IAAPA Tradeshow in Atlanta, Georgia, USA. The original train design was to have gold-flake strips and a single headline.

Ruth Voss, the park's public relations manager at the time, is credited with naming the new coaster in early 1979.

The ride's name was revealed on February 6, 1979. It would be called The Beast. Construction of the attraction was completed in March 1979.

The Beast opened at the start of the season on April 14, 1979 following an exclusive preview the day before.

The ride was modified in 1980 with two underground tunnels being connected into one longer tunnel and some banking adjusted from 25° to 40°.

Magnetic brakes were added to the ride in 2003.

For its 25th anniversary in 2004, The Beast was given a new soundtrack and visual audio effects.

In 2019, to celebrate the 40th anniversary of the attraction, Kings Island restored the original yellow color scheme of the trains, as well as the footprints that lead to the entrance.

On November 1, 2021, a project started to refurbish of track. The Gravity Group reprofiled the first drop and tunnel, the second drop, and the 540-degree helix. In-house carpenters replaced approximately of track in other areas, and the angle of the first drop was changed from 45 to 53 degrees to allow smoother transitions throughout the first tunnel. With the modifications, the ride's length changed from 7,359 feet to 7,361 feet. The Beast then reopened on May 8, 2022.

Kings Island worked with The Gravity Group again in 2025 to replace more than of track. The work impacted the ride's second hill and midcourse tunnel.

sections.title: Ride experience
sections.content: As the train rolls out of the station, riders make a right turn towards a mineshaft. The train makes a left turn and climbs a tall chain lift hill. While ascending the first lift hill, dramatic music plays on the speakers. An announcer says "Remain seated throughout the entire ride. Please keep hands and legs inside the car at all times. Thank you." The dramatic music continues to play until the train reaches the top. After holding for a few seconds, riders plunge into the first tunnel, reaching a max speed of. The tunnel is followed by a left turn as the train zooms through the forest. A right turn is followed by a long straight section of track. Riders turn right and head into the second tunnel. Prior to 1980, the second tunnel was separate, with the outdoor section being enclosed. After exiting the tunnel, the train hits a right turn. As riders hit a second right turn, they bank right up a hill and descend down a straight section, which leads into an another chain lift hill. The train climbs the lift hill as the announcer on speakers repeat the same thing as the first one. At the top, riders get a view of the park and the train hits an 18 degree bank, as it drops into a double helix with two tunnels added during the 1980 season. After this, riders make a left turn and return to the station.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: The trains were built by Philadelphia Toboggan Coasters. The park originally had four trains with five four-bench cars each. The ride was more efficient with only three trains, leading to the fourth train being used for spare parts. Within two to three years of opening, the cars were modified to three benches each.

sections.title: Rankings and Awards
sections.content: In October 2004, The Beast was given a Coaster Landmark Award by the American Coaster Enthusiasts club. There is a plaque outside the ride commemorating this.

fullText:
The Beast is a wooden terrain roller coaster located at Kings Island in Mason, Ohio, USA. It is currently the longest operating roller coaster in the United States, as well as the longest wooden roller coaster in the world. The ride takes up 35 acres ( km/h) of land. The trains were built by Philadelphia Toboggan Coasters.

Kings Island unveiled the roller coaster, at the time unnamed, on July 10, 1978. According to the park, the project had been in planning for three years.

Lead engineers Al Collins and Jeff Gramke, with some consulting assistance from legendary designer John C. Allen, headed the project. Charles Dinn, director of maintenance and construction, led construction efforts and enlisted help from Curtis D. Summers for the footings and helix finale. Late design modifications were made to resolve issues discovered during testing, delaying plans to build an enclosure over the helix until the following off-season.

A ride car was displayed at the 1978 IAAPA Tradeshow in Atlanta, Georgia, USA. The original train design was to have gold-flake strips and a single headline.

Ruth Voss, the park's public relations manager at the time, is credited with naming the new coaster in early 1979.

The ride's name was revealed on February 6, 1979. It would be called The Beast. Construction of the attraction was completed in March 1979.

The Beast opened at the start of the season on April 14, 1979 following an exclusive preview the day before.

The ride was modified in 1980 with two underground tunnels being connected into one longer tunnel and some banking adjusted from 25° to 40°.

Magnetic brakes were added to the ride in 2003.

For its 25th anniversary in 2004, The Beast was given a new soundtrack and visual audio effects.

In 2019, to celebrate the 40th anniversary of the attraction, Kings Island restored the original yellow color scheme of the trains, as well as the footprints that lead to the entrance.

On November 1, 2021, a project started to refurbish of track. The Gravity Group reprofiled the first drop and tunnel, the second drop, and the 540-degree helix. In-house carpenters replaced approximately of track in other areas, and the angle of the first drop was changed from 45 to 53 degrees to allow smoother transitions throughout the first tunnel. With the modifications, the ride's length changed from 7,359 feet to 7,361 feet. The Beast then reopened on May 8, 2022.

Kings Island worked with The Gravity Group again in 2025 to replace more than of track. The work impacted the ride's second hill and midcourse tunnel.



As the train rolls out of the station, riders make a right turn towards a mineshaft. The train makes a left turn and climbs a tall chain lift hill. While ascending the first lift hill, dramatic music plays on the speakers. An announcer says "Remain seated throughout the entire ride. Please keep hands and legs inside the car at all times. Thank you." The dramatic music continues to play until the train reaches the top. After holding for a few seconds, riders plunge into the first tunnel, reaching a max speed of. The tunnel is followed by a left turn as the train zooms through the forest. A right turn is followed by a long straight section of track. Riders turn right and head into the second tunnel. Prior to 1980, the second tunnel was separate, with the outdoor section being enclosed. After exiting the tunnel, the train hits a right turn. As riders hit a second right turn, they bank right up a hill and descend down a straight section, which leads into an another chain lift hill. The train climbs the lift hill as the announcer on speakers repeat the same thing as the first one. At the top, riders get a view of the park and the train hits an 18 degree bank, as it drops into a double helix with two tunnels added during the 1980 season. After this, riders make a left turn and return to the station.

The trains were built by Philadelphia Toboggan Coasters. The park originally had four trains with five four-bench cars each. The ride was more efficient with only three trains, leading to the fourth train being used for spare parts. Within two to three years of opening, the cars were modified to three benches each.

In October 2004, The Beast was given a Coaster Landmark Award by the American Coaster Enthusiasts club. There is a plaque outside the ride commemorating this.
